content.problemStatement:
You are given Chef's calendar for the next NNN days, defined as a binary string SSS of length NNN where Si=0S_i = 0Si​=0 means that Chef has a holiday on the ithi^{th}ith day from now, and Si=1S_i = 1Si​=1 means that Chef has to work on that day.

Chef wants to plan his vacations. For each vacation, Chef needs XXX consecutive holidays in his calendar. Obviously, he can only go on one vacation at a time.

Chef can take at most one extra holiday. That is, he can flip at most one digit in SSS from 111 to 000. If he does this optimally, what is the **maximum** number of vacations that he can go on?


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and XXX.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS of length NNN — Chef's schedule.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer — the maximum number of vacations Chef can take if he takes at most one more extra holiday.
constraints: The sum of NNN across all test cases does not exceed 2⋅1052 \cdot 10^52⋅105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 111: Chef can flip the 3rd3^{rd}3rd digit to make his calendar 000000100000010000001. This allows him to take 333 vacations in the first 666 days.
Test Case 222: Chef can flip the 3rd3^{rd}3rd digit to make his calendar 100010001000. This allows him to take one vacation using the last 333 days.
Test Case 333: Regardless of whether Chef flips the 3rd3^{rd}3rd digit or not, he can take at most 222 vacations.
